speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with survivor Bianca Israeli Kafeman-- that's I-S-R-A-E-L-I K-A-F-E-M-A-N-- in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, November 3, 1995. I am the interviewer, John Lash. This is an interview with survivor Bianca Israeli Kafeman-- I-S-R-A-E-L-I K-A-F-E-M-A-N-- in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, November the 3rd, 1995. I am the interviewer, John Lash, L-A-S-H. Bianca, would you please describe what your life was like before the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Kalisz, and this is in a part of Poland that was very much under the influence of German culture and German life. I don't remember too much before the war, because, as you know, I was very little. But something come in mind to me, because it was an impact in my life. And my father was a jeweler and a watchmaker. And we had a-- we had a store in the middle of the city. We lived not far away, and it was [? Kanonicka ?] Three, if I remember correctly. I probably was going to a school for a few hours. We had a very nice life there. My parents were quite affluent. And I really didn't know anything about what's going on around me, because I was an only child, and very much protected by my parents. I just remember a thing which happened in 19-- beginning of 1939. My uncle, who lived for many, many years in Germany, came-- was deported from Germany and came to our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told a story, stories about Germany in those days, and how he passed the border and he was beaten up by the Germans. And it was very strange to me. I remember sitting, listening to him, and I was just stunned. What's going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, one day-- I don't remember which day it was-- suddenly my parents sat glued to the radio. And I was playing around, and they were hushing me. Don't talk. Don't talk. We want to listen. And I, I was a very obedient child, so I, of course, stopped playing. And I heard a voice. And this voice just fascinated me, because he was screaming, screaming in a language that I didn't understand. And it was the speech of Hitler. And then, it's-- we-- I know that, one day, because of the many anti-Semites that there were in Kalisz, because of the background of Volksdeutsche and German. Being on the balcony, I saw people running and screaming, 'Jews out of Poland!' And it was so strange to me, I guess, and even in that age, to hear a Jew just being called a Jew. Because I never knew what a Jew means. I knew that my parents were people living in Poland, like Poles. I-- my-- it was a-- my mother light the candles, and this what I knew about, actually about Jewish upbringing. Nothing else, because-- maybe because of my age. But the first thing what I felt, it was 1939. 1939, we were displaced right away. We were sent out of the city to Lodz. We were walking, I remember, myself and my mother, because my father was taken to go to Warsaw, I think to defend Warsaw. This was, I think, an order or something. I don't know. And we were walking by foot from Kalisz to Lodz, by foot and by, I think, in the beginning, we had a carriage. And then they took it away. And the Germans were flying their planes very, very low, and shooting. I remember being in a forest. And the bullets were flying around. And the first thing that was my Jewish word that I heard from everybody else was, [GERMAN]. [SOB] We survived that, came to Lodz, stayed for a long time in my uncle's apartment. And after a short time-- I cannot tell times-- we came back to Kalisz back. And my father came back from Warsaw. And from then on it started. We were displaced from our own apartment, which was in a Polish area-- not many Jews were living there-- to a part of our city that more Jews were living. And we lived in a small apartment. And I remember once that every morning, I heard Juden raus. Juden raus! And my father, of course-- the men were going out to work, or they took them for some kind of chores, that I don't know what. My father was running down and standing, and they were taking him for the whole day. Sometimes they were coming. And they weren't satisfied with the number of people that they were taking. So they came out, and they searched the apartment for men. I remember one day, a man came, a German, and I was probably terrified. So I just was sitting there in a corner. And he came to me and said to me, don't be afraid. I am a father. I have a child like you. [SOB] And I don't know what came, that I hugged him and kissed him. And I said, I wish my father wouldn't be afraid. And this was-- I don't remember times. I don't remember. I don't remember dates. But one day, they came and took us away from this place that we lived for a while. They took us and gathered us in a marketplace. In this marketplace were all Jews and the intelligentsia, Polish intelligentsia. And we stayed there for-- I don't know how long-- not long. Probably a week. Not more than that. And one day, my parents told me, please be quiet. Don't say anything. Just come with us. And I went with them, and through the barbed wires of this camp, when the Germans were bringing all supplies very early in the morning. Somebody picked up the wire, and we crawled out of the camp, of this gathering place, and went into a buggy with this horse. And we went away. And I didn't know where we're going. I didn't know what's going on. And we came to a station. My father and mother told me to be, to be quiet, not to talk. We had already on us the yellow band. And I don't know how it happened, but we were on a train going to Warsaw. Apparently, Jews weren't allowed to go to the [INAUDIBLE], so we stood outside. And suddenly, a man, a German in uniform, called us in. And my mother-- and he didn't call my father, only me and my mother. And he called us in and told us to sit, and started to talk to my mother. And as a-- and there were another three Germans. And as they were talking-- my mother had a very big family in Germany. It happens that they knew her uncle. And they were very, very polite to us. They gave me chocolate-- I remember that-- and also convinced my mother, or tried to convince my mother to stay in Poland, then they will take care of us. But my parents probably had another plan. They wanted to escape Poland. And we came to Warsaw. And from Warsaw, again on the train, we went to Lublin. And I don't remember what other little town. And my parents were smuggled through the border to Russia. I remember very vividly, because I think this was my first encounter with death. We were running through the border. And in no man's land, the Germans were shooting on random. Just shooting in the air. Shooting. And I wore a little coat. And through this coat went out a bullet, went through a bullet. It didn't touch me. It's a shame that I don't have this coat with me. But it was a coat with a hole in it from a bullet. We finally crossed the border and came to Russia. We came to [SIGHS] to a town by the name of Lodz-- sorry-- Luck. And in this town, my father either-- I don't know how it happened. We lived in with a Jewish family, with very, very poor family. And I was playing with this little boy, living in a place without a window. And I was just playing with this boy, because I was small, and he was probably my age or a little bit older. And my parents, of course, had to go and earn a living. We were there for a short time, a few months, I think. That the Russians, one night, they came. And they put us into cattle wagons, and we went. For three weeks, we were in this cattle wagon and went to Siberia. We came to Siberia, to a camp. And in this camp-- I don't know-- we stayed very, very short time. But my-- I remember that when we came to this camp, to those barracks, the barracks were still like warm from people who were before. And apparently, those people were the Germans that they were before there, because they were also concentrated there. And we came in. It was a very remote place in Siberia. It's called taiga. And we stayed there for a short time. And we were transferred to another city-- not city, it was a little town. They don't even call it-- they don't even call it a town. They call it, like, a point. And it calls [RUSSIAN]. And it was not far away from Tomsk. And, again, we went to those barracks. And my father was working, and my mother was working. And I couldn't go to school because I didn't have shoes. So I stayed in this little room. We were eight or nine people living together, because-- and I was the only child. By the way, when we went on this barge from one-- from this taiga to [RUSSIAN], there was an outbreak of scarlet fever. And almost every child my age died. So I didn't even have many children my age, at that time, to play with. And those who were a little bit older went to school. And since I really didn't have what to wear on my feet, and it was very cold in Siberia, I had to stay home. And I stayed on my own. I probably was occupied with myself, making dolls, sometimes even making ready potatoes for those people who were coming late from work, peeling them. I remember peeling potatoes. I don't know how I did it in the age of six, peeling potatoes. I don't know how I did it. But I must be a good peeler. And I was also sick there. Because we had very little food, I had avitaminosis, which is like scurvy. I lost my hair. I had sores all over my body. But I survived that, somehow. I don't know. I didn't get any vitamins, because they didn't have any vitamins. But somehow my mother and father nursed me. And I was fine. We stayed there approximately, I think, a year and a half. And from there, we were released by the Polish government in exile. And we were free from there-- because this was, actually, not a guarded camp, but a working camp, a labor camp, for the grown-ups. And we went-- we were free, and we went to Asia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you to just go back a little bit. When you had to walk from your hometown to Luck, how far was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's about 120 kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything that happened in the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the journey, what I remember, it's always bombardment. Always running away. Always hiding, because the Germans were above us all the time, trying to gun down, I guess, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go on to your experience in the cattle car. You said you were in there for three weeks. Can you describe some of the things that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cattle car going to Siberia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that we had very little food. We had to wash ourselves on the-- I guess my mother was washing me there. I don't even remember. I know that we didn't have washrooms or anything like that, so we had to run under the-- under the cars and-- excuse me-- to, to do what we had to do. Food was very scarce. And, of course, you know, as usual in those places, is lice and sometimes even people were rude to each other. Because, in those-- in the circumstances like that, you can understand that everybody wants to survive, and tries to get the most out of everything. I don't remember much, because, sometimes-- I don't know-- the mind works different ways. You try not to remember many things.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, let's go on to where we left off on your way to Asia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the way to Asia? So we actually took also-- not a normal train-- a cattle train, and we went to Asia. And this wasn't-- we were already free. So we could go on a station and buy something if we-- not that it was much to buy in Russia at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the money to buy these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was working in Siberia. He was working, and, and they got some kind of money. I don't know what and how much. But he was working in the forest, cutting wood. And then, because he was a jeweler and a watchmaker, he was repairing, under his spare time, whenever he had spare time, and I think he got a little bit of money from that. I don't know. It was poverty, but I don't know. I really don't know. Now, on the way to, to Asia-- we went to middle Asia, which is Tajikistan. And it calls, the city calls Leninabad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leninabad. And then, we also-- I don't know if they rented, or how they got a room with, also, many, many people in it. I was only child always. Somehow, I was always among grown-ups. I never had anybody my age or even a little bit older. And we, again, lived together, like in a commune, in this big room. And my father got sick. He contracted typhoid. And he was very, very sick. And this was a very traumatic thing for us, because he was almost dying. And he was many, many months in the hospital, because it was complications. And then, we really didn't have almost anything to eat. And we-- because we lived with so many people, they tried to, to give us something, or whatever was left we, we got for ourselves. My father got better and came home and stayed also. And I remember teaching him how to walk. And he was quite a husky man, but he couldn't walk. I taught him how to walk. I taught him how to, how to chew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very, very tragic time in our lives, at that time. But he came-- he recuperated after a while, and started to work, and started to work as a, as a watchmaker. And he earned money, as much as we needed for, for us, for food. And we moved into a different apartment by ourselves already.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I must have been, already, about seven, eight years old. And I went-- they sent me to school, to a Polish school, which was also provided by the exile government in London, Polish government. And we-- I went to a Polish school, as I told you, and I was taught Polish and Russian. But going to school, the way to school wasn't so easy, because every morning we had to have some older children take us to school, because we were stoned by the natives, because we were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. Yes, it was Russia. It was Russia, and it was war. And I think the propaganda, the German propaganda, worked everywhere to, to ignite the Anti-Semitic vein that is even in the Russians, and the Tajiks more so. And in the school, of course, we were sitting, winter time, with gloves and everything, because our school was without windows. Not that it was very cold there, but it was-- it got quite cold in the winter time was quite cold. And the summer was very hot. But the winter time were quite cold, and we had to sit in our gloves, and-- and write in our gloves, because it was cold. So it was, also, you would think that the Russians are wonderful, were wonderful to us as neighbors. But going to school was really frightening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear any specific arm bands or anything, that would indicate you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They just knew. It wasn't such a big city. And they knew who was Jewish and who was not Jewish. And we stood up, maybe in our-- because of our features. I don't think so. But the-- the Asiats are different than we. They have different features of face. And they are more like Mongols. And we have normal-- you know, normal faces, like white people. So they knew. They knew, probably. There-- And I think it was in 1944 that my father was taken to a labor camp in Russia. They took all the men. I think it was at the time of Leningrad-- of Stalingrad. And they took all the men away, not only Jewish men, but all the men. And he was taken to a labor camp, again, somewhere in Siberia. I don't remember where. And he stayed there for many months. I think till, almost the war was, was finished. Almost, a few months before. And again, we [? struck ?] our poverty, because it was impossible to sell anything. And we were on our own. My mother was working, but couldn't provide us with enough money. We didn't-- I cannot say that, that we were afraid of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, let's stop here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
